era_context: Late 15th century: The Italian Renaissance is in full flourish; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power under Bayezid II; the Iberian Reconquista is nearing completion; the Ming Dynasty in China is stable; the Aztec Empire is expanding; European maritime exploration is accelerating along the African coast.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islander societies (no contemporary written record)

events.title: Diogo Cão begins second voyage of African exploration
events.description: Portuguese navigator Diogo Cão departs on his second voyage of exploration along the African coast in 1482, continuing the work of Portuguese maritime expansion southward. Cão's first voyage (1482–1484) had already extended Portuguese knowledge of the African coast, and his second voyage (1485–1486) would take him even further south, eventually reaching the mouth of the Congo River and establishing contact with the Kingdom of Kongo. The 1482 voyage marks the continuation of Portuguese efforts to find a sea route to India and to establish trading posts and territorial claims along the African coast. Cão's voyages were part of the systematic Portuguese exploration sponsored by King John II.

sources.name: Portuguese navigation records and royal archives
João de Barros, Décadas da Ásia (16th-century chronicle)

events.certainty_note: Diogo Cão's voyages are well-documented in Portuguese records, though the exact dates of departure and arrival are sometimes disputed. The 1482 date for the beginning of his exploration is supported by most sources.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Discovery
Precedes Bartolomeu Dias's rounding of the Cape (1487–1488)

events.title: Edward IV dies; succession crisis in England
events.description: Edward IV, King of England, dies on 9 April 1483 (not 1482, but the succession crisis that followed shaped events in 1482 and beyond). However, in 1482, Edward IV was still alive and ruling. The year 1482 saw Edward IV consolidating his power after the brief restoration of Henry VI (1470–1471) and the subsequent Wars of the Roses. Edward IV's reign was marked by the establishment of the House of York on the English throne and the beginning of the end of the Wars of the Roses. In 1482, Edward IV was in the final year of his life, and his death would trigger the succession crisis that led to Richard III's seizure of power.
events.certainty_note: Edward IV's reign dates are certain. His death in April 1483 is precisely documented.
cross_references: Part of the Wars of the Roses

events.title: Treaty of Arévalo between Spain and Portugal
events.description: The Treaty of Arévalo (also called the Treaty of Toledo) is signed in 1482 between Spain (the united crowns of Castile and Aragon under Ferdinand and Isabella) and Portugal (under John II). The treaty addressed disputes over territorial claims in North Africa and the Atlantic, particularly regarding Portuguese exploration along the African coast and Spanish interests in the Canary Islands and North Africa. The treaty reaffirmed Portuguese rights to explore and trade along the African coast, while Spain focused on the Canary Islands and the Reconquista in North Africa. The treaty was part of the broader Iberian competition for maritime expansion and colonial territory.
events.certainty_note: The treaty is documented in Spanish and Portuguese archives. The date of 1482 is supported by multiple sources.

events.title: Tizoc reigns as Aztec Emperor
events.description: Tizoc (r. 1481–1486) continues his reign as the eighth Aztec emperor in 1482. Tizoc's reign is characterized by military consolidation and the maintenance of tribute networks. In 1482, Tizoc was in the early years of his reign, overseeing the administration of the Aztec Empire and conducting military campaigns to maintain control over subject city-states. Tizoc's reign is less celebrated in Aztec tradition than those of his predecessors and successors, but it maintained the stability of the empire during a period of transition.
events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology for this period relies on later indigenous codices and Spanish-era compilations. The exact sequence of events in 1482 is approximate.

events.title: Chenghua Emperor rules Ming Dynasty
events.description: The Chenghua Emperor (r. 1464–1487) continues his reign over the Ming Dynasty in 1482. The Chenghua Emperor's reign is characterized by relative stability and cultural flourishing. In 1482, the Ming Dynasty was at the height of its power, with a strong bureaucracy, flourishing arts and literature, and extensive trade networks. The Chenghua Emperor's reign saw the continuation of the Great Wall's construction and maintenance, the consolidation of Ming control over China, and the development of Ming ceramics and porcelain.

sources.name: Ming Dynasty imperial records and archives

events.certainty_note: The Chenghua Emperor's reign dates are certain.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Arévalo's exact date is sometimes disputed; some sources place it in 1481 or 1483 rather than 1482. The Aztec chronology for 1482 is approximate and depends on later indigenous sources; no contemporary Aztec written records survive for this specific year.
historiographic_note: 1482 is well-documented in European sources (Portuguese exploration accounts, Spanish and Portuguese diplomatic records, English chronicles) but relies on later compilations for non-European regions. Aztec history depends entirely on post-conquest indigenous codices and Spanish-era ethnographic records. Chinese sources are abundant but often focus on court politics. African and Southeast Asian sources are sparse in European archives.

graph_edges.note: Diogo Cão's exploration continued with the erection of padrões along the African coast
The Treaty of Arévalo established precedent for Iberian maritime division, leading to the Treaty of Tordesillas
